text.title: aws-architecture-services Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for aws-architecture-services. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Choose Lambda when traffic is variable or spiky, execution is under 15 minutes, cold start under 2 seconds is acceptable, and GPU is not required.
rule.rationale: Lambda costs per invocation; idle time costs nothing. Containers cost per provisioned hour regardless of traffic.

rule.statement: Choose containers (EKS or ECS Fargate) for steady 24/7 workloads above 1 million requests per day, long-running processes, GPU requirements, persistent websocket connections, or when the Kubernetes ecosystem is needed.
rule.rationale: Reserved container capacity is cheaper than Lambda at high sustained throughput. GPU workloads require EC2-backed compute. Lambda has a 15-minute hard timeout and no persistent connections.

rule.statement: Default to a hybrid architecture: Lambda for event-driven and sporadic workloads, containers for always-on services. Document the boundary in an architecture decision record.
rule.rationale: Most teams run hybrid architectures. The right question is which workload belongs where, not a binary choice.

rule.statement: Choose Fargate (ECS or EKS) when the team lacks Kubernetes expertise or wants to avoid node management overhead.
rule.rationale: Fargate is fully managed; no patching, scaling, or capacity planning for nodes.

rule.statement: Choose EKS when multi-cloud portability, Helm ecosystem, GitOps with ArgoCD, or advanced scheduling is required.
rule.rationale: EKS uses standard Kubernetes APIs, enabling migration to other clouds or on-prem clusters.

rule.statement: Use RDS Aurora PostgreSQL for relational OLTP; use DynamoDB for key-value or document access patterns requiring single-digit millisecond latency at any scale; use Redshift for analytical queries over >1TB.
rule.rationale: Aurora provides storage auto-scaling and read replicas. DynamoDB avoids connection pooling limits at high concurrency. Redshift columnar storage is purpose-built for OLAP.

rule.statement: Use EventBridge for decoupled cross-service routing; use Step Functions for stateful orchestration; use SQS for async buffering with retry; use SNS for fan-out notifications.
rule.rationale: Each service solves a distinct coordination problem. Using SQS for routing or SNS for stateful workflows creates operational complexity.

rule.statement: Apply all six Well-Architected pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability.
rule.rationale: Skipping any pillar creates technical debt: missing Security leads to breaches; missing Cost Optimization leads to uncontrolled spend.

rule.statement: Default to Graviton (ARM64) instances for all new compute resources; require an explicit comment to justify x86 usage.
rule.rationale: Graviton provides roughly 20% lower cost and comparable or better performance for most workloads.

rule.statement: Use a custom event bus (not default) for application events; enable event archive with 30-day retention for replay capability.
rule.rationale: The default event bus mixes AWS service events with application events. Archiving enables replay during outages.

rule.statement: Always configure a dead-letter queue and retry policy on every EventBridge target rule.
rule.rationale: Without DLQ, failed event deliveries are silently dropped. EventBridge retries up to 185 times over 24 hours by default.

rule.statement: Use SQS Standard for async decoupling with at-least-once delivery; use SQS FIFO when message ordering matters; use SNS for fan-out to multiple subscribers.
rule.rationale: SQS provides buffering and retry; SNS delivers immediately to all subscribers without buffering.

rule.statement: Use Step Functions for workflows with more than 2 sequential steps, error handling, or human approval gates.
rule.rationale: Chaining Lambda functions directly via code creates invisible retry logic and poor observability. Step Functions provides visual state machines with built-in retry/catch.

rule.statement: Use EventBridge choreography for loosely coupled, independently deployable services; use Step Functions orchestration for multi-step transactions requiring compensation (saga pattern).
rule.rationale: Choreography reduces coupling but makes flow hard to trace. Orchestration centralizes visibility at the cost of coupling to the orchestrator.

rule.statement: Deploy EKS cluster in private subnets with IRSA enabled, secrets encryption via KMS, and managed node groups using Graviton instances.
rule.rationale: Private subnets prevent direct internet exposure. IRSA eliminates long-lived access keys on nodes. KMS encryption protects secrets in etcd.

rule.statement: Enable at least CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI, and EBS CSI driver as managed add-ons.
rule.rationale: Managed add-ons receive security patches automatically without manual intervention.

rule.statement: Always deploy Aurora with Multi-AZ (minimum 2 instances), encryption at rest via KMS, IAM authentication enabled, and deletion protection on prod.
rule.rationale: Single-instance Aurora has no automatic failover. Deletion protection prevents accidental data loss in production.

rule.statement: Set backup retention to 30 days in production; enable Performance Insights and Enhanced Monitoring with 60-second granularity.
rule.rationale: 30-day retention meets most compliance requirements. Performance Insights identifies slow queries proactively.

rule.statement: Redirect HTTP port 80 to HTTPS 443 with SSL policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06; enable access logs to S3 with 90-day lifecycle.
rule.rationale: TLS 1.3 policy disables weak cipher suites. Access logs are required for security audits and debugging.

rule.statement: Use CloudFront Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3 origins, not legacy OAI; enable HTTP/2 and HTTP/3.
rule.rationale: OAC uses SigV4 signing and supports SSE-KMS, which OAI does not.

rule.statement: Tag all resources with Project, Environment, Owner, and ManagedBy=terraform; enable CloudTrail (multi-region trail) and AWS Config in every account.
rule.rationale: Tags enable cost allocation and compliance reporting. CloudTrail provides the audit trail required for SOC2/PCI.

rule.statement: Use Spot instances for fault-tolerant batch workloads, Reserved capacity for steady-state, and Instance Scheduler to stop non-prod environments overnight.
rule.rationale: Instance Scheduler can save up to 75% on non-prod costs. Spot pricing is 60-90% cheaper than On-Demand for interruptible workloads.
